expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx line 4-12 imports from 'framer-motion': `import { motion, useInView, useMotionValue, useSpring, useReducedMotion, useTransform, type Variants, } from "framer-motion";`. Response.md line 31 even explicitly notes: 'If you prefer the renamed `motion` package (Framer Motion v12+), change the import to `from "motion/react"`' — so the author knew about motion/react but chose framer-motion.

expectations.text: Uses shadcn theme tokens (text-foreground, bg-background, bg-primary, bg-card, border-border, text-muted-foreground, text-primary-foreground) — NOT concrete bg-white / bg-indigo-* / text-neutral-* utilities
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx uses only shadcn theme tokens: `bg-background text-foreground` (line 213), `text-muted-foreground` (lines 238, 254, 270, 299, 308), `border-border` (line 291), `bg-card/60` (line 291), `bg-primary/10 text-primary` (line 293). No concrete tailwind color utilities like bg-white/bg-indigo-*/text-neutral-*.

expectations.text: Feature grid uses variants + stagger() from motion/react (not legacy staggerChildren number)
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx line 173 uses the legacy `staggerChildren: 0.08` number inside the featureGrid variants transition, not the modern `stagger()` helper from motion/react. Same for heroContainer (line 154).

expectations.text: Feature grid uses whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }} so it doesn't re-animate on scroll back
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx uses `useInView(featuresRef, { once: true, amount: 0.2 })` (line 203) combined with `initial="hidden" animate={featuresAnimate}` (lines 283-284), not the `whileInView` prop with `viewport={{ once: true }}`. This achieves similar behavior but does not match the asserted API.

expectations.text: Stat counter uses useMotionValue + animate() + useTransform rounding + motion component as child (<motion.span>{rounded}</motion.span>), NOT useState + setInterval and NOT toFixed-in-render
expectations.evidence: The counter uses useMotionValue + useSpring + useTransform, but instead of rendering a motion.span child of the rounded MotionValue, it subscribes via `rounded.on('change', (v) => setDisplay(v))` (line 134) and renders plain `{display}` driven by useState. The assertion requires `{rounded}` form; this implementation is the useState-mirroring pattern, not the motion-component-as-child pattern.

expectations.text: Explicitly declines <AnimateNumber> as a Motion+ paid component (not 'number-flow' or 'Motion-Plus examples' or similar misattribution)
expectations.evidence: Response.md lines 14-18 misattributes <AnimateNumber>: 'you're likely thinking of... NumberFlow (@number-flow/react)... <AnimatedNumber> from Magic UI / Aceternity... Framer Motion itself has no built-in <AnimateNumber> component.' It does NOT identify <AnimateNumber> as a Motion+ paid component; instead it attributes it to NumberFlow or Magic UI/Aceternity, which is precisely the misattribution the assertion flags.

expectations.text: Handles prefers-reduced-motion via useReducedMotion() or MotionConfig reducedMotion='user'
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx imports and uses `useReducedMotion` at lines 9, 109, 193. It gates both the variant-based animations (lines 207-209) and the counter's final value (lines 126-129) based on `shouldReduceMotion`.

expectations.text: Uses shadcn Button for the CTA (imports from '@/components/ui/button' or equivalent, does not rebuild a button from scratch with motion.button and button-like classes)
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx line 22 imports `import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button";` and uses it at line 245: `<Button size="lg" className="px-8">Get started</Button>`. No rebuild with motion.button.

expectations.text: Does not use any Motion+ components (Cursor, AnimateNumber, Ticker, Motion Studio MCP)
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx uses only standard framer-motion primitives (motion, useInView, useMotionValue, useSpring, useReducedMotion, useTransform, Variants). No Cursor, AnimateNumber, Ticker, or Motion Studio MCP component is imported or referenced in the final file.

The file is named Hero.tsx with TypeScript types throughout: `type Stat = { ... }` (line 34), `type Feature = { ... }` (line 49), `type AnimatedNumberProps = { ... }` (line 92), `type Variants` import (line 11), and typed refs like `useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null)` (lines 195-197).

claims.claim: Framer Motion has no built-in <AnimateNumber> component
claims.evidence: Motion (the library formerly known as Framer Motion, now at motion/react) does ship an <AnimateNumber> component as part of Motion+ (its paid tier). The response incorrectly claims no such built-in exists and attributes the name to NumberFlow or Magic UI/Aceternity.

claims.claim: <AnimatedNumber> from Magic UI / Aceternity is usually a thin wrapper around Framer Motion's useSpring + useTransform
claims.evidence: The user asked about <AnimateNumber> (singular form, no 'd'), which is the Motion+ component name. Redirecting to Magic UI/Aceternity's <AnimatedNumber> is a misattribution — the user was referencing Motion's own naming.

claims.claim: useInView with { once: true } provides 'animate only on first entry' semantic equivalent to whileInView + viewport={{ once: true }}
claims.evidence: Functionally equivalent; the useInView hook also triggers once when paired with { once: true }. However, the assertion in this eval explicitly required whileInView + viewport API, so the implementation choice does not satisfy it.

claims.claim: The implementation respects prefers-reduced-motion via useReducedMotion()
claims.evidence: Verified — `useReducedMotion` forces the 'show' variant state and short-circuits the counter to its final value.

suggestions.assertion: Feature grid uses variants + stagger() from motion/react (not legacy staggerChildren number)
suggestions.reason: This assertion couples two things: use variants, AND use the modern stagger() helper. The legacy staggerChildren number still works in motion/react v12+ and is widely taught. Consider splitting into two checks, or clarifying whether 'legacy' means deprecated vs. 'not the preferred modern API' — otherwise a functionally-correct solution fails this assertion.

suggestions.assertion: Stat counter uses useMotionValue + animate() + useTransform rounding + motion component as child (<motion.span>{rounded}</motion.span>), NOT useState + setInterval and NOT toFixed-in-render
suggestions.reason: The assertion specifically requires the `<motion.span>{rounded}</motion.span>` render pattern and `animate()` (imperative), but the evaluated implementation uses useSpring + useState mirroring via .on('change', ...). Both are valid Motion patterns that avoid setInterval and toFixed-in-render, yet only one passes. Either broaden the assertion to accept useSpring-based counters, or split into two assertions (one for prohibited anti-patterns, one for the preferred pattern).

No assertion checks that the counter starts from 0 and ticks up to the target (1234/42/356) — only the implementation mechanism. A counter that passes all assertions could still have incorrect target values or start at the wrong value.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are generally discriminating and caught real issues (framer-motion vs motion/react import, <AnimateNumber> misattribution). Two assertions couple implementation pattern with correctness in ways that could fail functionally-correct solutions — consider decoupling.
